abstract:
Knowledge graph embedding aims to represent entities and relations in a continuous low-dimensional vector space to facilitate efficient link prediction and reasoning. While TransE has emerged as a state-of-the-art method due to its simplicity and scalability, it struggles to effectively model complex relational properties, such as reflexive, one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many mappings. Conversely, existing models that can capture these complexities often suffer from high computational costs and limited scalability. 

To address these limitations, we propose TransH (Translating on Hyperplanes), which models each relation as a translation operation on a relation-specific hyperplane. This approach allows an entity to have different distributed representations depending on the relation it is involved in, significantly enhancing the model's capacity while maintaining the computational efficiency of TransE. Furthermore, we introduce a new training strategy that utilizes mapping properties to weight the construction of negative samples, thereby reducing the prevalence of false negatives in incomplete knowledge graphs. We evaluate TransH on benchmark datasets including WordNet and Freebase for tasks such as link prediction, triplet classification, and relational fact extraction. Performance results demonstrate that TransH consistently outperforms TransE and other baseline models, offering a superior balance between predictive accuracy and scalability.
